query: What is the difference between id and class selectors in CSS?

competitors.label: Selectors and combinators
competitors.selectionReason: This is the best MDN canonical source for the relationship between class and ID selectors, their syntax, and how they participate in compound selectors.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on selector syntax, compound selectors, and the formal difference between class and ID selectors.
theirGaps: The guide is less interview-shaped and less explicit about the production pitfalls around duplicate IDs, reusable UI, and specificity debt.

realWorldUseCases: An id is unique on a page and selected with #id; a class can be reused and selected with .class. IDs have higher specificity and are often used for anchors or JS hooks, while classes are for styling groups.
followUpConfusion: IDs are unique identifiers for single elements, while classes group multiple elements under a shared style.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains the selector differences clearly, but it is lighter on the practical production trade-off between unique hooks and reusable styling systems.
actionableExamples: It includes direct examples of class, ID, and compound selectors, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the next mistake teams hit in practice: duplicate IDs in reusable UI and overusing IDs for styling instead of classes.

actionableExamples: Keep the styling example visible so the answer stays concrete instead of purely conceptual.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical selectors guide, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it turns id-versus-class into a concrete uniqueness, specificity, and reusable-UI decision.

followUpConfusion: IDs are unique identifiers for single elements, while classes group multiple elements under a shared style.
